Notebook 1 · notebooks/01_setup_baseline.ipynb
01THE BIG PICTURE

What is this notebook trying to do?

What we did
  • Defined the task: read a text, predict one of 7 conditions — anxiety, bipolar, depression, normal (no condition), personality disorder, stress, suicidal.
  • Cleaned the data (two dedup passes), then locked one stratified train/val/test split to disk.
  • Measured what "no skill" scores (random, majority) and what "simple rules" score (two keyword classifiers).
  • Prepared a human benchmark: 70 blind texts waiting to be labelled.

Why it matters
  • The project's central question — how well can we do with only 32 labeled examples? (Part 2) — is meaningless without reference points.
  • Comparisons are only fair if every model takes the same exam — hence one fixed, guarded test set.

021a · CONTEXT

What does good performance even look like?

What we did
  • Reviewed the literature and collected benchmarks for mental-health text classification (table below).
  • Identified the business case: chatbots that flag early signs of anxiety/depression, and triage tools that help clinicians prioritize — the WHO counts ~970 million people living with a mental disorder.
Why it matters
  • Classical ML (Logistic Regression, SVM) reaches ~87% — then hits a ceiling.
  • LSTMs with static embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe) fail badly — F1 near 0% on some classes (Hasan et al., 2025) — because a static embedding gives a word one meaning regardless of context.
  • BERT reads the sentence in both directions and consistently lands 90%+. The 96.6% on our dataset is the Part 3 target.
  • All published benchmarks use the full dataset — what survives with 32 labels (Part 2) is an open question.
The numbers
ModelTaskAccuracyF1Reference
BERT fine-tunedOur exact dataset96.6%—Elite13, HuggingFace
BERT fine-tuned7-class mental health94.8%94.8%Sevinç, arXiv 2025
RoBERTa7-class (Reddit)—0.91–0.99Hasan et al., arXiv 2025
BERT-base7-class (Kaggle)90.0%—Springer, 2026
BERTBinary mental health95%0.95Hrnjić & Altoka, IJCA 2026
LogReg + TF-IDFBinary mental health92%0.92Hrnjić & Altoka, IJCA 2026
Logistic Regression7-class mental health86.8%87.0%Sevinç, arXiv 2025
031b · LOAD & EXPLORE

Getting to know the data

What we did
  • Loaded sai1908/Mental_Health_Condition_Classification (HuggingFace, MIT license) at a pinned revision — a Hub update can never silently change our numbers.
  • Profiled class balance, text lengths, and token counts with BERT's own tokenizer.
  • Built word clouds and sampled examples per class — from the training split only, so nothing derived from them ever saw the test set.
Why it matters
  • Balance: biggest class (anxiety, 17.0%) is only 1.6× the smallest (suicidal, 10.7%) → no resampling tricks needed, and Part 2's 32-example sample can cover every class.
  • The "normal" class means the model must also recognize when nothing is wrong — a harder, more realistic task.
  • Length is a signal — and a problem: normal texts are strikingly short (~18 words); depression (175) and suicidal (152) are the longest and will lose the most to BERT's 512-token cut. Stress / personality disorder / bipolar / anxiety sit at similar lengths — between those, only meaning can separate them.
  • Vocabulary overlap is the central difficulty: anxiety, depression and suicidal share "feel", "want", "mind", "trapped" — "I feel trapped and want to escape" fits all three. Every result below comes back to this.
  • Label noise is proven, not suspected: 24 texts appeared under conflicting labels (one labeled both depression and suicidal; the Excel artifact #name? under four classes), and the near-duplicate pass found 56 more conflicting groups. Undetected boundary cases surely remain → an accuracy ceiling no model can pass.
The numbers
34

82

ClassMedian tokens% over 512 tokens

041b · CLEAN

Cleaning: two dedup passes before anything else

What we did
  • PASS 1 Exact problems: conflicting labels (24 texts / 50 rows — all copies dropped) · normalised duplicates (zero exact text+label duplicates, but 1,592 texts identical once case/whitespace are normalised; 1,566 counted disjointly from conflict/junk, first copy kept) · near-empty texts ≤5 chars (58). Total: 1,674 rows.
  • PASS 2 Near-twins: texts sharing a 200-character normalised prefix count as the same document (the conflicting pair above differed by one character: 794 vs 795 chars). Conflicting prefix groups dropped (56 groups / 123 rows) + same-label near-duplicates (1,582).
  • Normalisation = lowercase + collapsed whitespace — exactly what BERT's uncased tokenizer ignores, so these duplicates are identical to the model.
Why it matters
  • A twin on both sides of the split leaks answers — like finding the exam questions in your homework. Every metric silently inflates.
  • Dedup after splitting can't fix it: the twins are already on both sides. It must happen first.
  • Before the fix: 463 test texts (2.27%) had a near-twin in train/val. After: 0 — and a verification cell re-checks on every run, failing loudly if leakage ever reappears.
  • The 56 extra conflicting-label groups from pass 2 double as more hard evidence of label noise (→ section 03).
The numbers
Raw
103,488
Removed (3.27%)
3,379
Clean
100,109

Proof the leak mattered: the same keyword classifier scores 67.04% on the old (leaky) test set vs 66.90% on the clean one. Small for rules — for a model that memorizes (BERT), the inflation would be far worse.

051b · SPLIT

One split, three jobs, locked forever

What we did
  • Split the cleaned data stratified (each part keeps the same 7-class proportions) with fixed SEED = 42.
  • Persisted everything to data/processed/: three parquet files, labels.json, and a manifest with a SHA-256 fingerprint of the test IDs.
  • Added a split guard: on every re-run the recomputed split must match the fingerprint, or the notebook refuses to overwrite anything.
  • Verified zero near-duplicate leakage across the split (see section 04).
Why it matters
  • Train = the textbook. Validation = practice quizzes used for every tuning decision. Test = the final exam, sat exactly once per frozen model. Mixing them is cheating.
  • The guard protects Parts 2–4: if a library update ever changed the split function's behavior, we'd find out immediately — instead of silently invalidating every saved result.
  • Stratification makes the test set a fair miniature of the whole dataset.
The numbers
64,069
Train (64%) — models study these
16,018
Validation (16%) — all tuning decisions
20,022
Test (20%) — touched once per frozen model
061c · BASELINE A

Random & majority — the performance floor

What we did
  • Computed the random-guess expectation two independent ways: analytically (Σ over classes of P(test class) × P(predicted class) = 14.53%) and by simulating 1,000 random classifiers (14.54% ± 0.24%). They agree — the floor is rock-solid.
  • Added the majority baseline: always predict the most frequent class ("anxiety").
  • Persisted both to results/metrics/ so Part 3's comparison table loads them instead of retyping numbers.
Why it matters
  • Each trivial strategy games one metric: majority wins accuracy (16.7% > 14.5%) but collapses on macro F1 (0.041 — it ignores six classes); random wins macro F1 (0.143). That's exactly why every result reports both.
  • Cohen's kappa (κ) joins the metrics here: agreement after subtracting chance. κ = 0 means guessing — the majority baseline scores exactly 0. It can't be gamed by exploiting class frequencies.
The numbers
14.5%
random accuracy (± 0.24%) · macro F1 0.143
16.7%
majority accuracy · macro F1 0.041 · κ = 0
071d · BASELINE B OLD METHOD

Hand-crafted keywords — no learning, just word lists

Definition. A human picks keywords per class by inspecting training examples and word clouds. To classify: count how many distinct keywords of each class appear (on word boundaries — so "happy" can't fire inside "unhappy", or "mania" inside "Romania"), predict the class with the most matches. Ties → most distinctive vocabulary wins. No match at all → predict normal, the class defined by the absence of distress vocabulary.
What we did
  • Wrote 12–19 keywords per class (e.g. mania, mood swings → bipolar; deadlines, overwhelmed → stress; kill myself → suicidal).
  • Tuned on validation only: V0 (59.9%) → expanded V1 (66.6%, adopted) · occurrence counting (+0.08pp, rejected — not worth it) · list-size normalisation (64.6%, rejected — hurt).
  • Froze the winner, scored test exactly once; saved all 6,627 errors to results/rule_based_errors.csv as a hard-cases set for Parts 2–3.

The two rejected-variant numbers come from the tuning round on the earlier (pre-prefix-dedup) split; the notebook keeps the design conclusion without re-running them.

Why it matters
  • The catch-all trade-off: no-match texts default to normal → recall 0.98 but precision 0.43. A default class always buys recall by donating its errors. Anxiety shows the mirror image (precision 0.76, recall 0.65): anxious texts without explicit vocabulary drain into normal.
  • Distinctive vocabulary wins, overlap loses: stress (F1 0.85) and bipolar (0.82) at the top; depression (0.46) and suicidal (0.49) at the bottom — exactly what the word clouds predicted.
  • Context-blindness is the hard limit: "I want to die laughing" vs "I want to die" — no word list can tell them apart.
The ethics number: suicidal recall = 0.41. 59% of suicidal texts are missed — 876 land in normal (no keyword fired: the most dangerous failure), 229 in depression. Every later model is judged on this number, and it's why we frame the task as screening support, not diagnosis.
The numbers
66.9%
accuracy (95% CI 66.3–67.6) 4.6× random
0.670
macro F1 (95% CI 66.4–67.6)
0.611
Cohen's κ
ClassPrecisionRecallF1Verdict
stress0.880.820.85strong
bipolar0.950.710.82strong
personality disorder0.970.640.77precise, decent recall
anxiety0.760.650.70overlaps
normal0.430.980.60over-guessed catch-all
suicidal0.610.410.49confused
depression0.600.370.46confused

History: the first notebook version reported 59.7% — original keywords (V0) on the old, leaky test set. Heads-up if presenting from the notebook: markdown cells 6, 19 and 24 still quote superseded numbers (rewrite queued); the cell outputs and this guide carry the current ones.

081d · BASELINE C NEW METHOD

Mined lexicons — let statistics write the word lists

Definition. Rank every word and two-word phrase in the training split by how statistically distinctive it is for each class — using the z-scored log-odds with a Dirichlet prior (Monroe et al., 2008). In plain language: compare how often a term appears in one class vs the rest (log-odds), smooth every count toward the corpus average so rare words can't win by luck (prior), and divide by the uncertainty so only reliably distinctive terms rank high (z-score). Keep the top K per class; classify exactly as before (count matches → argmax → normal if nothing fires). The only change vs the old method is who wrote the lists.
What we did
  • Mined unigrams + bigrams from train (terms in <20 documents ignored), scored the whole vocabulary per class in one vectorised pass.
  • Tuned the lexicon size K on validation only (grid 25 → 1600), froze K = 400, evaluated test once.
  • Ran McNemar's paired test against the hand-crafted classifier on the same 20,022 test texts.
Why it matters
  • The mined terms are interpretable: "whirlwind", "worry", "fear" → anxiety; "one moment", "the next", "energy" → bipolar (the mood-swing contrast, found automatically); "kill myself", "to die" → suicidal.
  • The depression/suicidal overlap is visible in the lexicons themselves: both rank "do not", "i am", "i do", "cannot" near the top — generic first-person negation. That's the mechanism behind depression's over-prediction below.
  • The normal lexicon is casual function words ("you", "it's", "we") — there is no "normal vocabulary"; the everyday register is itself the signal.
  • The failure pattern moved, it didn't disappear: normal stopped being the catch-all (precision 0.43 → 0.74) — but depression took over (precision 0.44, recall 0.78), absorbing the ambiguous distress texts. Better keywords relocate the ambiguity; they can't remove it.
  • Still context-blind: "I want to die laughing" fools this one too → the gap BERT must close.
Suicidal recall: 0.41 → 0.45. Better, still the weakest class — even statistically optimal word lists miss 55% of suicidal texts, because the suicidal/depression boundary is genuinely blurry at the word level.
The numbers
74.5%
accuracy (95% CI 73.9–75.1) +7.6pp vs old
0.749
macro F1 (95% CI 74.4–75.5)
0.701
Cohen's κ
K (terms/class)25501002004008001600
Val accuracy67.4%67.9%72.3%73.8%74.7%75.1%75.7%
ClassPrecisionRecallF1vs old F1

McNemar on the paired test predictions: mined-only correct on 4,224 texts, hand-crafted-only on 2,701 → p ≈ 2.5×10⁻⁷⁵. The same paired test will compare BERT variants in Part 3.

10HUMAN BENCHMARK ACTION PENDING

How good are humans at this task?

What we did
  • No published human benchmark exists for this dataset, so we built one: a stratified sample of 70 test texts, shuffled, written without labels to results/human_eval/human_eval_sample.csv.
  • Gold labels live in a separate key file — not to be opened until annotation is finished.
To do (the one open Part 1 task)
  • Fill the human_label column for all 70 rows — blind, no peeking at the key.
  • Re-run the scoring cell → it reports human accuracy + macro F1 and saves part1_human_level.json. Status: 0/70 labelled.
Why it matters
  • If a careful human scores ~80%, the gap between 74.5% and 100% is mostly irreducible label ambiguity, not model weakness — and the realistic BERT target shifts accordingly.
  • It's the concrete answer to the examiner question "how do you know the labels are even right?"

What Part 1 accomplished
  • Dataset pinned to a fixed revision; label noise documented (80 conflicting-label groups).
  • 3.27% of rows removed in two dedup passes before splitting; zero leakage, verified on every run.
  • One stratified 64/16/20 split locked behind a SHA-256 fingerprint.
  • Four baselines with CIs + kappa, and a significance test (McNemar) between the top two.
  • A 70-text human benchmark ready to score, and 6,627 hard cases saved for error analysis.
What happens next
  • Part 2: BERT fine-tuned on just 32 labeled examples — can a context-aware model beat a 74.5% keyword baseline with almost no supervision?
  • Then: data augmentation, zero-shot LLM, LLM-generated training data — all scored on this same test set.
12Q&A BANK

Questions and answers

Why deduplicate before splitting, not after?
Dedup after splitting can't help — the twins are already on both sides of the fence. Before splitting, every text appears once, so train and test are guaranteed disjoint. We verified it: 0 test texts share a 200-char normalised prefix with train/val.
You tuned your keywords and your K — doesn't that contaminate the test set?
No: every tuning decision (keyword expansion, rejected design variants, the K grid) was evaluated on the validation split only. The frozen classifier touched test exactly once. The test numbers are one-shot estimates.
Why report accuracy AND macro F1 AND kappa?
Each catches a different way to cheat: majority-guessing inflates accuracy (16.7%) but not macro F1 (0.04); macro F1 treats all classes equally regardless of size; kappa subtracts chance agreement entirely (majority scores κ = 0). A genuinely good model must clear all three.
How do you know mined lexicons are really better and not just lucky?
McNemar's paired test on identical test texts: mined-only-correct on 4,224 texts vs hand-crafted-only-correct on 2,701, p ≈ 2.5×10⁻⁷⁵. The improvement is statistically decisive.
Why freeze K at 400 when K=1600 scored higher on validation?
Diminishing returns plus interpretability: past a few hundred terms the "rule set" stops being readable and converges toward a full bag-of-words model — at that point you should just train one (Parts 2–3). K=400 keeps the baseline honest to its purpose: a transparent, inspectable rule set.
What's the weakest point of the current results?
Suicidal recall: 0.45 at best. The classifier misses over half of suicidal texts, mostly sending them to normal or depression. In a screening application that's the most expensive possible error, which is why we track this single number through every later model.
Couldn't the labels themselves be wrong?
Some demonstrably are: 24 texts carried conflicting labels exactly (one labeled both depression and suicidal; #name? under four classes), and the near-duplicate pass found 56 more conflicting groups. Those were removed, but undetected boundary cases surely remain — that's why we're building a human-level benchmark to estimate the irreducible-ambiguity ceiling.
Why is this framed as screening, not diagnosis?
Ethics and honesty: a text classifier has no clinical context, misses 55% of suicidal texts, and its errors are not random. It can help triage — surface texts for human review — but presenting it as diagnosis would be irresponsible. This framing should appear in the executive summary too.
13GLOSSARY

Terms used above, in one breath each

PrecisionOf the texts the model called class X, how many really were X? ("When it speaks, is it right?")
RecallOf the texts that really are class X, how many did the model catch? ("Does it miss people?")
F1The harmonic mean of precision and recall — high only when both are high.
Macro F1F1 computed per class, then averaged with equal weight — big classes can't mask failure on small ones.
Cohen's kappa (κ)Agreement with the true labels after subtracting chance agreement. 0 = guessing, 1 = perfect.
95% CI (bootstrap)Resample the test set 1,000 times and recompute the metric; the range covering the middle 95% shows how much the number could wobble from test-set sampling alone.
Stratified splitA split that preserves the class proportions in every part — each part is a fair miniature.
LeakageTest information sneaking into training (e.g. near-duplicate texts on both sides). Inflates scores silently.
Log-odds (z-scored, Dirichlet prior)A statistic ranking how distinctive a term is for one class vs the rest, smoothed so rare words can't win by luck, scaled by its uncertainty.
McNemar testA paired significance test on the texts where two classifiers disagree — answers "is model A really better than B on this same test set?"
TokenThe unit BERT reads (≈ word pieces). BERT processes at most 512 per text; longer texts get truncated.
